narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid LSAT score of 36 and a middle-income family background, your law school application stood out in some ways, including your plan to attend full-time like most students. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated an 80% chance that you would fail it— a threshold that led to your rejection, as the school prioritizes applicants predicted to succeed. This prediction was driven primarily by your academic performance in school, particularly your grades in later years. The most influential factor was your position in the second decile for Year 3 grades, which is notably lower than the typical student's fifth or sixth decile ranking and strongly pushed the model toward predicting bar failure. Next in importance was your Year 1 decile ranking, also in the second decile compared to the average around the sixth, further reinforcing the risk of struggling on the bar. Your undergraduate GPA of 2.80, below the dataset average of 3.21, added to this concern by nudging the prediction toward failure, though less forcefully than your school deciles. In contrast, your LSAT score was close to the average and helped somewhat toward a passing prediction, but it wasn't enough to offset the top concerns. Overall, your profile compares to typical students by having competitive test scores and a common full-time status, but your consistently lower decile rankings in school grades set you apart as higher risk for bar success. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—many factors like family income played neutral roles here—but the model weighs early and later academic deciles heavily for this prediction. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your school deciles, aiming for top-half rankings that align with successful bar passers. You might also strengthen your undergraduate record through post-baccalaureate options or highlight any upward trends in a future application. With targeted improvements in these academic areas, you could shift the model's view and open doors at other schools. Keep pushing; your LSAT shows you have the aptitude to build on.
